attributes.stem:
A 39-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a many-year history of visible varicose veins affecting his left leg (Image 1). He seeks treatment because he finds them unsightly and they have gradually enlarged. He reports no pain, heaviness, itching, skin changes, bleeding, or prior superficial thrombophlebitis. There is no history of deep vein thrombosis. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works in an office and is otherwise physically active.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Body mass index is 24 kg/m2. There are prominent, tortuous superficial veins along the medial aspect of the left calf with a visible below-knee course; the veins decompress with direct pressure and leg elevation (Image 2). There is no ankle oedema, no hyperpigmentation, no lipodermatosclerosis, and no ulceration. Peripheral pulses are intact and there are no signs of arterial disease.

Duplex ultrasonography of the left leg shows axial reflux of the below-knee great saphenous vein with segmental varicosities; there is no deep venous thrombosis and no significant perforator incompetence.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
Management of primary lower limb varicose veins is guided by symptoms, cosmetic concerns, and duplex ultrasound findings. In otherwise healthy people with confirmed saphenous trunk reflux, minimally invasive endothermal ablation has become the preferred definitive treatment in Australia because it closes the incompetent axial vein with high success, low complication rates, and rapid recovery. Compression hosiery can provide symptom relief but does not correct axial reflux in candidates suitable for intervention.

Endovenous laser ablation is most appropriate here because duplex confirms great saphenous vein reflux with cosmetically troublesome varicosities in a healthy person. Endothermal ablation of the incompetent trunk, often combined with ambulatory phlebectomy or adjunct foam sclerotherapy for tributaries, provides durable outcomes and addresses the pathophysiology of axial reflux.

Reassurance alone may be reasonable for truly asymptomatic, non-progressive veins in those declining treatment, but this person actively seeks a cosmetic solution and has documented reflux that can be definitively treated, making observation alone suboptimal.

Compression stockings can reduce aching and oedema and may be used if intervention is unsuitable or declined. However, he has minimal symptoms and primarily cosmetic concern, and compression does not abolish reflux or the visible varicosities; it is inferior to a definitive endovenous option in this context.

Surgical ligation without addressing the incompetent saphenous trunk is inadequate and associated with higher recurrence because reflux persists. Modern care targets the refluxing axial vein rather than isolated ligation of tributaries.

High ligation and stripping is an effective historical operation for saphenous reflux, but compared with endothermal ablation it has more postoperative pain, longer recovery, and higher wound complications. In a healthy person with suitable anatomy, endovenous ablation is preferred.
learningPoints: People who seek treatment for cosmetically concerning varicose veins should first have duplex ultrasound to define reflux patterns and guide therapy.
Endothermal ablation of an incompetent saphenous trunk is first-line for symptomatic or cosmetically bothersome varicose veins due to axial reflux.
Compression hosiery can relieve symptoms but does not correct underlying venous reflux in people suitable for definitive intervention.
references: Atkins E, Mughal NA, Place F, Coughlin PA. Varicose veins in primary care. BMJ. 2020 Jul 7;370:m2509. Available from: https://www.bmj.com/content/370/bmj.m2509.long

Ojha V, Kumar S. Current strategies for endovascular management of varicose veins: An updated review of superficial ablation technologies. Phlebology 2022; 37:86 –96. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34505546/

Kemp N. A synopsis of current international guidelines and new modalities for the treatment of varicose veins. Aust Fam Physician. 2017;46(4):229-233. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2017/april/a-synopsis-of-current-international-guidelines-2
